content.text: When an internal or external audit is announced, control owners face a structured demand: assemble evidence, demonstrate that controls operated as designed across the audit period, respond to Prepared By Client (PBC) requests, and attend auditor walk-throughs. Without a playbook, each control owner reinvents the process under time pressure, producing inconsistent evidence packages and avoidable findings.
: an auditor finding should never be the first time a control owner discovers a gap in their evidence. The playbook's job is to surface that gap at T-60 days, not during fieldwork.
Audit preparation is a three-party activity across the three lines of defence. Each lane has distinct responsibilities and hand-offs:

attrs.alt: Control Owner Audit Preparation Playbook swim-lane diagram

content.text:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

class PlaybookStatus(str, Enum):
    NOT_STARTED  = "not_started"
    IN_PROGRESS  = "in_progress"
    READY        = "ready"        # evidence gathered; self-assessment complete
    SUBMITTED    = "submitted"    # PBC response sent to auditor
    FINDING_OPEN = "finding_open" # auditor raised a finding on this control
    CLOSED       = "closed"       # finding resolved or no finding raised

@dataclass
class PBCItem:
    """One line item from the auditor's Prepared By Client list."""
    pbc_id: str            # e.g. "PBC-014"
    control_id: str
    description: str       # what the auditor is asking for
    due_date: date
    response_files: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)   # S3 keys or file names
    response_notes: str = ""
    submitted: bool = False

@dataclass
class ControlAuditPlaybook:
    """
    Per-control prep checklist owned by the control owner.
    Created when the audit notice is received and tracked until findings close.
    """
    control_id: str
    control_title: str
    owner: str             # IAM principal / name
    audit_period_start: date
    audit_period_end: date
    status: PlaybookStatus = PlaybookStatus.NOT_STARTED
    self_assessment_passed: Optional[bool] = None
    evidence_s3_prefix: str = ""
    pbc_items: list[PBCItem] = field(default_factory=list)
    findings: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    notes: str = ""

    def open_pbc_count(self) -> int:
        return sum(1 for p in self.pbc_items if not p.submitted)

    def is_ready(self) -> bool:
        return (self.self_assessment_passed is True
                and self.open_pbc_count() == 0
                and self.status not in (PlaybookStatus.FINDING_OPEN,))

The self-assessment is the control owner's own verification that evidence is complete before the auditor sees it. Automate every verifiable check; flag manual attestations that require human sign-off:
Example Checks for the PII Filter Control

import httpx
import boto3
from datetime import date, timedelta

def build_pii_filter_checks(control_id: str) -> list[SelfAssessmentCheck]:
    """Self-assessment checks for the PII output-filter control."""
    return [
        SelfAssessmentCheck(
            check_id=f"{control_id}-A1",
            description="PII filter control test ran at least once in the audit period",
            automated=True,
            verify_fn=lambda: check_test_ran_in_period(control_id,
                                                        date.today() - timedelta(days=90),
                                                        date.today()),
        ),
        SelfAssessmentCheck(
            check_id=f"{control_id}-A2",
            description="All control test results in the period have PASS outcome",
            automated=True,
            verify_fn=lambda: all_tests_passed(control_id,
                                                date.today() - timedelta(days=90),
                                                date.today()),
        ),
        SelfAssessmentCheck(
            check_id=f"{control_id}-A3",
            description="Evidence package exists in S3 with valid SHA-256 manifest",
            automated=True,
            verify_fn=lambda: evidence_package_valid(control_id),
        ),
        SelfAssessmentCheck(
            check_id=f"{control_id}-M1",
            description="Control design documentation is current (reviewed in past 12 months)",
            automated=False,  # requires human attestation
        ),
        SelfAssessmentCheck(
            check_id=f"{control_id}-M2",
            description="No known exceptions or waivers active for this control",
            automated=False,
        ),
    ]

def check_test_ran_in_period(control_id: str, start: date, end: date) -> bool:
    """Query the control test log for at least one run of this control in the period."""
    import psycopg2
    conn = psycopg2.connect(dsn="postgresql://audit_ro@db/controls")
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute(
        "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM control_test_log "
        "WHERE control_id = %s AND tested_at BETWEEN %s AND %s",
        (control_id, start, end),
    )
    count, = cur.fetchone()
    conn.close()
    return count > 0
When the auditor issues a PBC list, each item maps to one or more evidence artefacts. The builder retrieves artefacts from the evidence store and bundles them with a signed index:

import zipfile, io, json, boto3
from pathlib import Path

class PBCResponseBuilder:
    """
    Assembles a PBC response package: collects the requested artefacts
    from the evidence store, bundles with an index, and produces a ZIP
    ready to share with the auditor via a secure portal.
    """
    def __init__(self, evidence_bucket: str):
        self._s3 = boto3.client("s3")
        self._bucket = evidence_bucket

    def build_response(self, pbc_item: PBCItem, playbook: ControlAuditPlaybook) -> bytes:
        buf = io.BytesIO()
        with zipfile.ZipFile(buf, "w", zipfile.ZIP_DEFLATED) as zf:
            index = {
                "pbc_id":       pbc_item.pbc_id,
                "control_id":   pbc_item.control_id,
                "description":  pbc_item.description,
                "prepared_by":  playbook.owner,
                "prepared_at":  date.today().isoformat(),
                "notes":        pbc_item.response_notes,
                "files":        [],
            }
            for s3_key in pbc_item.response_files:
                obj = self._s3.get_object(Bucket=self._bucket, Key=s3_key)
                content = obj["Body"].read()
                filename = Path(s3_key).name
                zf.writestr(f"evidence/{filename}", content)
                index["files"].append({
                    "filename": filename,
                    "s3_key":   s3_key,
                    "size_bytes": len(content),
                })
            zf.writestr("pbc_index.json", json.dumps(index, indent=2))
        pbc_item.submitted = True
        return buf.getvalue()
The PBC List — What Auditors Ask For
For a GenAI system, a typical PBC list covers these categories:
Policy document, control specification, flow diagram
Test run log for the audit period; pass/fail with evidence SHA
Stratified sample of inference log records; guardrail-triggered records
Deployment history; approval records; model cards
Reviewer decision log; approval rate; escalation log
IAM policy export; list of principals with prod access; access reviews
Current SOC 2 / ISO 27001 report for LLM vendor
List of incidents in the audit period; resolution status

Auditors typically request a walk-through: a live demonstration that the control works as documented. Prepare a script so the walk-through is consistent and does not rely on the control owner improvising:

# walk_through_script_CTR-001_pii_filter.yaml
control_id: CTR-001
control_title: PII Output Filter
walk_through_duration_minutes: 30

steps:
  - step: 1
    title: "Show control in architecture"
    description: >
      Open the inference gateway architecture diagram. Point to the PII filter
      component and explain: it sits between the LLM output and the response
      serialiser. Every response passes through it before being returned.
    artefact: architecture_diagram_v3.png

  - step: 2
    title: "Live demonstration"
    description: >
      In the staging environment, submit a prompt that includes an SSN pattern.
      Show the response is blocked or the SSN is masked. Show the guardrail_triggered
      field in the inference log record.
    artefact: staging_demo  # live system; no file

  - step: 3
    title: "Control test evidence"
    description: >
      Open the test_pii_filter_active result from the most recent CI run.
      Show: outcome = PASS, evidence SHA matches the stored artefact,
      and the artefact contains the blocked HTTP response body.
    artefact: ci_test_result_CTR-001_latest.json

  - step: 4
    title: "Log sample"
    description: >
      Open the pre-prepared log sample (PBC-007). Walk through 3 records:
      one where guardrail_triggered=false (normal), one where it is true
      with action=blocked, one where it is true with action=redacted.
    artefact: pbc_007_log_sample.zip

  - step: 5
    title: "Exception handling"
    description: >
      Explain what happens when the filter service is unavailable:
      the gateway returns a 503 rather than passing the unfiltered response.
      Show the circuit-breaker configuration and a historical 503 log entry.
    artefact: circuit_breaker_config.json
At T-30 days, the risk steward runs a readiness sprint across all control owners:

content: for the audit period. Every control with zero evidence records gets an immediate remediation task.
: each control owner submits their self-assessment results. The risk steward reviews all FAIL and NOT_ATTESTED items.
: simulate the PBC list using last year's actual PBC or an internal template. Control owners respond as if it were real; the risk steward marks completeness.
: for the five highest-risk controls, conduct a rehearsal walk-through with the control owner. Note any gaps in the script or live demo.
: any gap identified in T-30 to T-14 is logged as a pre-audit finding with an owner and due date.

content.text: Despite thorough preparation, auditors may raise findings. A finding is not a failure — it is an obligation to respond. Each finding requires a management response within a defined window (typically 10 business days):
Control design or operating gap, limited impact
Root cause + remediation plan with milestone dates
Material control failure; potential regulatory exposure
Immediate containment + root cause + remediation plan + risk register update
Same issue raised in a prior audit and not resolved
Evidence of prior remediation attempt; revised plan with escalated ownership
Automate playbook creation and status tracking so control owners receive tasks, not emails:

def initialise_playbooks_from_audit_notice(
    audit_notice: dict,   # {period_start, period_end, controls_in_scope, pbc_list}
    control_owners: dict[str, str],  # control_id → owner
) -> list[ControlAuditPlaybook]:
    playbooks = []
    for ctrl in audit_notice["controls_in_scope"]:
        pbc_items = [
            PBCItem(
                pbc_id=item["pbc_id"],
                control_id=ctrl,
                description=item["description"],
                due_date=date.fromisoformat(item["due_date"]),
            )
            for item in audit_notice["pbc_list"]
            if item["control_id"] == ctrl
        ]
        playbooks.append(ControlAuditPlaybook(
            control_id=ctrl,
            control_title=audit_notice["control_titles"].get(ctrl, ctrl),
            owner=control_owners.get(ctrl, "unassigned"),
            audit_period_start=date.fromisoformat(audit_notice["period_start"]),
            audit_period_end=date.fromisoformat(audit_notice["period_end"]),
            pbc_items=pbc_items,
        ))
    return playbooks

content: : a log file in S3 is not evidence until its SHA-256 has been confirmed and the content has been reviewed for the expected records. Many owners collect files but don't open them.

content.text: Walk-through dependent on a specific person

content: : if the control owner is unavailable during fieldwork, the auditor cannot proceed. Document the walk-through script so any team member can deliver it.

content.text: Responding to PBCs after the deadline

content: : late PBC responses delay fieldwork and signal poor process maturity to auditors. Track each PBC due date in the playbook and escalate 3 days before.
: control owners who have not pre-drafted a management response template take too long to respond to findings, sometimes missing the response window.
: owners who jump straight to evidence collection without a self-assessment often discover major gaps only during the auditor walk-through.

content.text: A control owner audit preparation playbook converts the audit process from a reactive scramble into a predictable, repeatable programme. Start at T-60 days with a self-assessment against the control specification, run a coverage report to surface evidence gaps, and rehearse the walk-through script before fieldwork begins. Automate every verifiable self-assessment check; require explicit attestation for the rest. When findings are raised, have a management response template ready and validate it before submission. The goal is that every item on the auditor's PBC list can be fulfilled within 24 hours — because the evidence was gathered, verified, and indexed months before the auditor asked for it.
